critique.summary: The article discusses a Twitter user’s observation that the model used in Cursor Composer 2 appears to be similar to Kimi K2.5 with RL, suggesting a possible rebranding or model reuse. It also notes the official release of Cursor Composer 2 in Cursor.
mainPoints: A Twitter user identified a model ID indicating Cursor Composer 2 may be based on Kimi K2.5 with RL.
The user suggests renaming the model ID for clarity.
Cursor Composer 2 has been officially released and announced on Twitter.
critique.truthfulness: The claims are based on a user’s observation of model IDs and an official announcement of Cursor Composer 2. The identification of the model as Kimi K2.5 with RL is plausible given the model ID, but there is no independent verification provided. The official release statement is factual, but the inference about the model's similarity remains speculative without further technical details.
considerations: Lack of detailed technical verification of the model's architecture or training data.
No official confirmation from the developers about the model's basis or rebranding.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Cursor Composer 2 being a repackaged version of the Kimi model with reinforcement learning, raising concerns about originality, licensing, and business ethics. Several commenters debate the legitimacy of Cursor's approach, the quality of their autocomplete model, and the potential consequences of licensing violations.

comments.author: gillesjacobs
comments.text:
Cursor is mostly an IDE / coding-agent harness company. So it probably makes sense for them not to train their own base model, but instead license something like Kimi and fine-tune it for their own harness and workflows.

Their moat looks pretty thin. A VSCode fork with an open-source LLM fork on top. In the fast-moving coding-agent market, it’s not obvious they keep their massive valuation forever.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced view on Cursor's business model and acknowledges the practical approach of licensing and fine-tuning rather than training from scratch.

comments.author: mohsen1
comments.text:
Cursor Composer 1 was Qwen and this is Kimi. IDE is based on VSCode. The entire company is build on packaging open source and reselling it.

Ollama is also doing this.

There is so much money to be made repackaging open source these days.

So funny to see Twitter go wild saying "a 50 person team just beat Anthropic" blah blah.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the reuse of open-source components and highlights a broader industry trend of repackaging open source software.

comments.author: deaux
comments.text:
Looks like two Moonshot employees confirmed that it's not licensed before Moonshot made the decision to get out of the debate and delete their posts [0][1].

[0] https://chainthink.cn/zh-CN/news/113784276696010804 - may have originally been https://x.com/apples_jimmy/status/2034920082602864990

[1] https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HD2Ky9jW4AAAe0Y?format=jpg&name=...
comments.analysis: This comment references insider information and deleted posts, suggesting licensing issues, which is plausible but less verifiable without direct evidence.

comments.author: rvz
comments.text:
Honestly I don't think this leak is any good for Cursor. Not only this appears as a violation to Moonshot's ToS, this may also be in fact enough evidence for Anthropic to ban Cursor from using their models, just like they are doing to OpenCode.

Why? As I said before, Anthropic mentions Moonshot AI (Maker of the Kimi models) as one of the AI labs that were part of this alleged "distillation attack" [0] campaign and will use that reason to cut off Cursor, Just like they did to OpenAI, xAI and OpenCode.

Let's see if the market thinks Composor 2 is really that good without the Claude models helping Cursor. (If Anthropic cuts them off).

[0] https://www.anthropic.com/news/detecting-and-preventing-dist...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a plausible consequence of licensing violations and contextualizes it within industry actions, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: prodigycorp
comments.text:
There are many reasons to make fun of Cursor. However, one of the things get right is their autocomplete model.

Are there any open models that come close? Why doesnt OAI or Anthropic dedicate some resources to blowing Cursor's model out of the water? Cursor's completion model is a sticking point for a lot of users.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights the quality of Cursor's autocomplete model and raises valid questions about competition and innovation in the field.

comments.author: simonw
comments.text:
I'm annoyed that we still don't know for certain which base model they used for Cursor 1.

This feels really rude to me. I have no problem with them fine-tuning open weight models to create their own - they are getting great results, and Cursor's research term should be respected for that. But deliberately hiding the base model they use is disrespectful of the researchers who created that model.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration about transparency and respect for original researchers, which is a reasonable ethical concern.

comments.author: Sammi
comments.text:
As a paying customer, it just doesn't feel good that they are trying to pass off someone else's model as their own.

I mean I guess this is what businesses do all the time. There's a term for it even, it's called white-labeling.

But is this all that Cursor have? They pass of VS Code as their own, they pass off Kimi as their own... What do Cursor even do? What do I need them for?
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a customer's perspective on business ethics and raises valid concerns about transparency and value proposition.

comments.author: HeavyStorm
comments.text: There's no "just" in RL. Fine tuning is very important and could make a lot of difference.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly emphasizes the significance of reinforcement learning and fine-tuning in improving model performance.

comments.author: agluszak
comments.text: A hyped startup providing zero added value, burning investor money only to repackage somebody else's work? That's new... /s
comments.analysis: This comment is sarcastic and dismissive, lacking constructive insight but reflecting skepticism about the startup's value.

comments.author: mono442
comments.text: This whole ai stuff feels like a big bubble especially with the oil price soon at $200 and guaranteed recession.
comments.analysis: This comment is speculative and off-topic, linking AI industry concerns to unrelated economic factors without evidence.
